name: Cova de ses Arrels
access: Own vehicle or AUMASA bus company, Tel.: (+34) 971.55.07.30
description: Discovered during road construction near Porto Cristo in January 2011, this cave features beautiful formations, two small lakes, and abundant tree roots that inspired its name. The main chamber measures approximately 30x27 meters with impressive white columns, stalactites, and stalagmites. It is considered a site of biological, speleological, archaeological, and paleontological interest.
full_description:
NEWS ABOUT THE DISCOVERY

On January 22, 2011, the newspaper "ULTIMA HORA" published the news that during the Porto Cristo bypass road construction works, a cave near the "Coves dels Hams" had been brought to light. Given such news, and before anyone thought of committing some monstrosity with it — on Sunday, February 27, two media outlets once again reported on the appearance of the cave, mentioning the discovery inside the cavity of Myotragus bones and pre-Talayotic pottery.

CONSEQUENCE OF THE DISCOVERY

Members of the FBE, led by their president Guen Mulet, went to verify the finding to see what exactly it consisted of, and once they confirmed that it had a certain significance, they immediately communicated it to the Department of the Environment (Medi Ambient), which authorized the FBE itself to carry out the terrestrial and underwater topographic survey as well as a study of the cave, all accompanied by the archaeologist and geologist from the construction company.

FIRST CONTACT WITH THE CAVE

On January 29, unaware of what is described in the previous paragraph, V. Pardo and I decided to make a first visit to make contact with the finding, not knowing whether we would find ourselves alone in the cave or would coincide with other groups who had also taken an interest. Indeed, a cave of considerable size was found at the indicated location, so we set to work with its exploration as well as carrying out initial photographic work. A week later, specifically on February 12, this time accompanied by R. Minguillón, T. Mateu, and F. Gutiérrez, we went to the site with the idea of scrutinizing any nook that we might have missed the previous week and to begin the topographic work of the cavity. On February 26, the same members as the previous occasion set out to finish the exploration work as well as the photographic work, which we completed after a seven-hour working day.

DESCRIPTION

The entrance to the cavity is less than four meters wide by approximately one meter high, and once we enter it, the height decreases further before we can recover our upright position in the "Entrance Chamber" or "Root Chamber," measuring about 20 x 10 meters, from whose ceiling hangs a great quantity of beautiful roots. Someone, after seeing a photo of the chamber, commented to me how interesting it is to see how roots develop exploring the space in the absence of supports and obstacles. The chamber is heavily decorated with different types of formations that are partly hidden by the large quantity of roots mentioned above. At the northeastern end of the chamber, a small low gallery that does not exceed ten meters descends on a gentle slope.

Once the visit to the "Root Chamber" is finished, we can continue the visit descending the steep earth ramp, though free of danger, until reaching the small lake (photo 4), measuring 10 x 5 meters with a maximum depth of 1.5 meters and 27 meters from the entrance, which separates us from the continuation of the cave, although once crossed, the cavity only continues on a gentle ascent for another five meters. Only noteworthy are the efflorescences found during our visit. The surroundings of the lake are covered with various lithochemical formations, among which stands out a "vegetal stalagmite" — in reality it is a stalagmite that the roots embrace, covering it as they grow seeking the moisture they cannot find inside the earth; some more specimens can be found in the cave.

To continue the visit, we can begin the ascent up the ramp toward the exit while scrutinizing from side to side. A sort of crawlway at the southeastern end of the cave gives us false hopes of continuity since it is impossible to pass through its narrowness. About nine meters from the exit to the southeast, a low passage opens the way for us to continue the exploration. The first thing we encounter is a sort of mini chamber where continuity opens through a narrow crawlway of considerable slope that deposits us on the ground almost facing another crawlway that for now we will ignore since we will use it on the return to exit through it.

We are at the beginning of the cave's largest chamber, with dimensions of approximately 30 meters in length by almost 27 at its widest point. The direction of the chamber is southeast and it begins once we pass through a sort of doorway formed by both stalagmitic barriers. A gentle slope heading southwest covers almost the entire chamber, forming floor flowstones in some places, and the rest of the surface is covered with dusty earth and stones of different sizes. In another place in the chamber and near the beginning of the descent to the small lake, stone blocks accumulate next to each other, the result of a ceiling collapse. The northeastern side, which is closest to the outer surface, is practically covered with stalagmites and columns, making it a difficult place to traverse, although it does not prevent access through one end to the cave's other small entrance, inaccessible to humans but not to some rodent that for a time used it to bring into the interior an immense quantity of snails on which it fed. The entire chamber is beautifully decorated with gorgeous columns of intense white color; of the same color are the interesting stalactites that hang from the ceiling in whimsical shapes; stalagmites of all sizes also adorn the cave floor, among which stand out some small ones that are covered with roots and which I usually call, perhaps erroneously, "vegetal stalagmites."

A small jump takes us into a small gallery of approximately 3.5 meters wide with low ceilings that in at least one place do not reach 40 centimeters. Once past the first jump, it is easy to appreciate certain corners adorned with elegant speleothems. The end of the gallery is marked by a small lake that barely exceeds one meter in width by a little more than half a dozen in length. In both this small lake and the larger one, a fine layer of calcite covered the water's surface at the time of our visits.

On the return to the exterior, once the main chamber is left behind, instead of climbing through the steep ascending narrow passage, we can do so through the extremely narrow crawlway that will deposit us right above the impassable passage near the lake. This area, at least in winter, tends to be wet and slippery, so care must be taken not to slip. The rest is ascending toward the exit.

Given the proximity to the neighboring tourist cave of "Hams," it would not be surprising if they are part of the same system, and although they are not connected by land, neither are they through either of the two lakes. Only the small one harbored hopes since on the last visit of February 26, 2011, we found a guide line tied to a stalagmite left by cave divers. Finally, the FBE informed us that the connection with the "Hams" caves through the lake was not possible.

STATE OF CONSERVATION

The state of conservation of the cave is acceptable, although it certainly seems to make clear that the cavity had been visited prior to its exposure by the bypass road works. It is impossible that in such a short period of time since the publication of the news on January 22 in the newspaper "ULTIMA HORA" and our first visit on the 29th of the same month, it would show so many signs of having been visited.

FAUNA

In a quick glance through the cave, the fauna located inside consisted of different species of arachnids and orthopterans; in the latter case, these were some young crickets.

SPELEOGENESIS

This is not the field in which I can move best since I lack training in this area. I will only say that the "Cova de ses Arrels" appears to be in an advanced evolutionary state in which lithochemical processes are abundant throughout, mainly in the descent area and near the main lake, also near the small mouth of the cave. Ceiling collapses can be appreciated in the southeastern area of the cavity with a group of blocks of considerable size.

TO CONCLUDE

In summary, an interesting cavity of considerable dimensions, full of formations, with two small lakes, several narrow passages that will give us the opportunity to get dirty from top to bottom, and above all noteworthy is the great quantity of roots of all types and sizes that invade the entire cavern and which suggested to us the name for the cave.

The "Cova de ses Arrels" can be considered a site of biological, speleological, archaeological, and paleontological interest.

The day's outing can be completed by making a visit to the "Cova de sa Figuera" which is located nearby, although you will need to drive to get there.
